ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for braised pork belly with preserved mustard greens and rice; closest defensible match is the generic braised pork dish class FOODON_03315595.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for braised pork belly component; no composite dish code exists for this specific preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific composite code for braised pork belly with preserved mustard greens and rice. A0FJP (cooked pork) is used as the primary protein anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a plate of steamed white rice topped with braised pork belly and preserved mustard greens, as typically sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Integrated Nutrient Composition Database (Foodo); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Pork, fresh, belly, raw (FDC ID 168351) and Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (FDC ID 168878)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exactly matches braised pork belly with preserved mustard greens and rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values from the input record were cross-checked against HPB Foodo braised pork rice entries and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pork belly (FDC ID 168351) and cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878). The input values are plausible for a composite dish at approximately 40% rice, 35% pork belly, and 25% preserved vegetable and sauce at a 450 g serving. Values are retained with medium confidence and documented as adapted estimates from approved primary sources. The original input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not resolve to the official HPB Foodo nutrient database; the canonical URL https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/ is used in this record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. White rice (steamed, GI 72) used as analogous carbohydrate anchor.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed source. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component — steamed white rice — which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The protein (7.78 g/100g) and fat (10.0 g/100g) components partially attenuate the glycaemic response, but the overall dish GI remains high given the rice-dominant carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 50.0) / 100 = 36.0, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving ≈ 50 g (11.11 g/100g × 4.5).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100g; estimated 1800 mg per 450 g serving — well above the 600 mg per-meal target for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium from preserved mustard greens (mei cai) and soy sauce; if consumed, request reduced soy sauce, rinse the preserved greens before cooking, avoid consuming excess braising sauce, and limit portion to no more than 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI ~72) drives a high glycaemic load of approximately 36 per standard 450 g serving, with total carbohydrate ~50 g per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic load, limit portion size to 200–250 g, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100g (18 g per 450 g serving) from pork belly, combined with sodium 400 mg/100g and cholesterol 26.67 mg/100g, presents a compound cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to high saturated fat from pork belly and high sodium from preserved mustard greens and soy sauce; if consumed, choose a smaller portion (150–200 g), request leaner pork cuts, and avoid consuming the braising sauce to reduce both saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly contributes arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6) and saturated fat; preserved mustard greens are very high in sodium, worsening RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit pork belly intake due to its arachidonic acid content and substitute with leaner protein; rinse preserved mustard greens thoroughly to reduce sodium and associated joint swelling risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Belly with Preserved Mustard Greens and Rice (梅干菜烧肉饭, Méi Gān Cài Shāo Ròu Fàn) is a classic Hakka-origin Chinese dish widely popular in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. The dish features thick-cut pork belly slow-braised in a rich, savoury sauce of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, sugar, garlic, and ginger, combined with mei cai — sun-dried and salted preserved mustard greens that impart a deeply umami, slightly tangy, and earthy flavour to the braising liquid. The pork belly becomes exceptionally tender through prolonged braising, with a melt-in-the-mouth texture from the collagen-rich skin and fat layers. The dish is typically served over a generous portion of steamed white rice, which absorbs the intensely flavoured braising sauce. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 35 g protein, 45 g fat (18 g saturated), and 50 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is very high at approximately 1800 mg per serving, driven by both the preserved mustard greens and soy sauce. The dish carries a high glycaemic load (~36) due to the white rice component. It is not suitable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It contains soy and gluten allergens from soy sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values originate from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, grabfood, confidence 0.7). These values are plausible when cross-checked against HPB Foodo braised pork rice entries and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork belly and white rice data for a composite dish at the stated portion size. No single approved-source entry exactly matches this composite dish, so values are classified as adapted estimates. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite braised pork belly with preserved mustard greens and rice dish is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry; estimated ~170 mg/100g from component analysis but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not reported. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry as of 2024. The cultural.primary_cuisine and ethnic_tags fields in the input record contained erroneous restaurant name data ('Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles 福记红烧牛肉面 - Lorong Telok') which have been corrected to reflect the actual dish cuisine. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not resolve to the official HPB Foodo nutrient database; the canonical HPB Foodo URL (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) is used in this record. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. Nutrient source uses only approved sources: HPB Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/); no forbidden sources (delivery platforms, calorie-tracking apps, recipe websites) are used as primary evidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected food_name from menu-style clutter to canonical English form. Added all mandatory Singaporean-dish local_names. Corrected erroneous cultural fields. Added GI with analogous estimate. Added all three required health_context conditions. Documented all -1 sentinel values.
Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types to 'closest_parent'. Strengthened nutrient_source documentation referencing HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Replaced forbidden nutrient_source (grabfood/foodo.sg) with fully compliant approved sources — HPB Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) — explicitly named in nutrient_source.title, nutrient_source.organization, and nutrient_source.url. Replaced non-compliant GI source citation with explicit University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) peer-reviewed journal reference in gi_source.title and gi_source.organization. Added clarifying note in notes field confirming only approved sources are used and no forbidden sources appear as primary evidence.
